title: Chocolate Mousse
subtitle: Dark chocolate folded into beaten eggs and set without gelatine
excerpt: Good dark chocolate melted with butter, combined with beaten yolks and sugar, then folded through whipped egg whites. Set in the fridge for two hours. No cream, no gelatine, the structure comes entirely from the eggs. The result is intensely flavoured and lighter than it looks.
sourceNotes: Chocolate mousse in the form of beaten eggs and melted chocolate appears in French cookery from the late nineteenth century onward, though the name 'mousse au chocolat' was not consistently used until the early twentieth century. The version here, using egg whites rather than cream as the primary aerating agent, is closest to the classic French preparation.

content.text: The chocolate should be at least 70% cocoa for this mousse, anything lower produces a sweeter result that tastes noticeably less intense after chilling. Melt the chocolate over a bain-marie rather than directly in a pan: direct heat makes chocolate seize and go grainy. The bowl must not touch the water, and the water must not boil.

The egg whites must be folded rather than stirred into the chocolate mixture. Folding preserves the air you have beaten in; stirring collapses it and makes the mousse dense. Use a large metal spoon and work in a figure-of-eight motion, cutting through the mixture rather than turning it. A few white streaks remaining is fine, they will disappear as the mousse sets.

Set a heatproof bowl over a saucepan of barely simmering water, making sure the bowl doesn't touch the water. Add {{dark-chocolate}} of dark chocolate and {{unsalted-butter}} of butter. Stir occasionally until melted and smooth. Remove from the heat and cool for 5 minutes.
Whisk 4 egg yolks with {{caster-sugar}} of caster sugar in a bowl until pale, thick, and ribbon-like when the whisk is lifted. Stir into the cooled melted chocolate.

Whisk the 4 egg whites in a clean, dry bowl until they hold firm peaks. Stir one large spoonful of the whites into the chocolate mixture to loosen it. Then add the remaining whites in two additions, folding gently with a large metal spoon in a figure-of-eight motion. Stop as soon as the whites are incorporated, small streaks are acceptable.
Spoon into six small glasses or bowls. Refrigerate for at least 2 hours until set. Serve as it is, or with a small amount of whipped cream on the side.

items.fix: Make sure the bowl is completely dry and the water below is barely simmering, not boiling. If the chocolate seizes, try adding a small splash of warm water and stirring vigorously, it sometimes recovers.
items.cause: Water got into the chocolate, or the heat was too high
items.symptom: Chocolate seized and became grainy when melting

items.fix: Beat the whites until they hold firm peaks and fold, not stir, into the chocolate. Work gently but decisively. Overmixing deflates the mousse.
items.cause: Egg whites were stirred rather than folded, or they were not beaten to firm enough peaks
items.symptom: Mousse is dense and heavy rather than light

items.fix: Let the chocolate cool until it is the same temperature as your hand before folding in the whites. A warm chocolate mixture will begin to cook the egg whites on contact.
items.cause: Chocolate was too warm when the whites were folded in, which cooked the whites slightly
items.symptom: Mousse is still liquid after 2 hours in the fridge

items.fix: A few white streaks are fine and will disappear as the mousse sets. Large white patches mean the whites were added in too large a lump, fold more thoroughly next time.
items.cause: Whites were not fully incorporated before chilling
items.symptom: White streaks remain visible after chilling

content.text: Chocolate mousse is a French pudding that spread around the world during the twentieth century. Beating eggs with chocolate shows up in French cookery writing from the late nineteenth century. The version with whipped whites for lift, rather than whipped cream, is probably older. It reached British home cooking after the war, when French cooking was the gold standard. By the 1970s it was a dinner-party staple. It is one of the few classic restaurant puddings that is just as good, or better, when made at home.
